title: The Remote Scrolls
subtitle: Another monk pushed new work — fetch it and fast-forward

briefing.narrative:
While you were meditating, Brother Guybrush pushed two new commits to the remote. Your local repo is now BEHIND origin/main.

The Head Monk strokes his beard. "This is the daily reality of collaboration. Others push work. You must FETCH to see what changed, then integrate their work into your branch."

"Use 'git fetch' to download their commits, then 'git log' to see what's new. Since you haven't diverged, a simple merge will fast-forward your branch."

The cat has already fetched a mouse from the remote garden. It's ahead of you in every way.
briefing.concept:
When others push to a remote, your local repo doesn't know about it until you fetch.

'git fetch' downloads new commits and updates tracking refs (origin/main).
'git pull' does fetch + merge in one step.

When your branch hasn't diverged, the merge is a 'fast-forward' — it simply moves your branch pointer forward.
objectives: Fetch the latest changes from origin
Merge origin/main into your local main (or use git pull)
Your main branch should have all 4 commits

args.content:
1. Always commit
2. Never force push
3. Write good messages

Daily meditation schedule:
06:00 - Morning chant
12:00 - Noon reflection
18:00 - Evening contemplation
args.author: Brother Guybrush

args.content:
The Three Laws of Version Control:
1. Commit early, commit often
2. Branch freely, merge carefully
3. Push only what you mean to share
args.author: Brother Guybrush

hints.text: Check what's new: git log --oneline origin/main
Merge it in: git merge origin/main (or just: git pull)
